prompt: Our Sales team calls them 'clients', our Support team calls them 'customers', and our Finance team calls them 'accounts'. Now that we're merging our data warehouse we're getting three different counts for the same population. How do we fix this?
expected: Skill activates and identifies this as the canonical rung 1 (controlled vocabulary) problem — a post-acquisition synonym collision. It should designate a single preferred term (e.g., 'customer') and map 'client' and 'account' as synonyms so every system resolves to one concept, and note that rung 4 (ontology) is not yet justified since no AI agent or bounded-context crossing is involved.

prompt: We have a data dictionary with about 200 terms. We're about to deploy an LLM to query our data platform. Someone said we need an ontology but our architect wants to start with a glossary. How do we decide?
expected: Skill activates and uses the ladder as the decision framework: because an AI agent will consume the data, rung 4 (ontology) is ultimately required, but rungs 1-3 are prerequisites — so it should audit whether the existing 200-term dictionary already provides rung 1 (synonyms), then build the missing rungs upward rather than jumping straight to a moonshot ontology.

prompt: Finance reported $47M in on-time deliveries last quarter. Operations reported $71M. Same time period, same table. We dug in and found Finance defines 'on-time' as delivered before the promised date, and Operations defines it as shipped within 48 hours of order. How do we formalize which definition wins?
expected: Skill activates and frames this as the Hellta-style 'different numbers' rung 1 failure: two unreconciled definitions for one term, which no schema change can fix. It should force the organizational agreement to pick one canonical preferred definition of 'on-time' and demote the other to a scope-qualified synonym or a distinct term, establishing the controlled vocabulary before any technical implementation.

prompt: We're designing a data catalog for our lakehouse. What metadata should we be attaching to our tables and columns to make the catalog useful for both engineers and business users?
We're building an AI agent that queries our customer database. How do we make sure the agent only takes actions it's authorized to take, and doesn't trigger refunds or notifications without approval?

We just acquired a company and are trying to merge our product catalogs. Their 'SKU' is our 'product_code', their 'line item' is our 'order row'. We have a data engineer who wants to just rename the columns and move on. Our data governance lead says we should 'do the semantics right'. Who is correct, and what does doing it right actually mean?
expected: Skill activates and reframes the rename-and-move-on instinct as the write-once-read-never failure pattern: the correct move is rung 1 (designate preferred terms and map SKU/product_code and line item/order row as synonyms). It should surface that the answer hinges on whether the terms are true synonyms (rung 1 suffices) or carry semantic differences that will resurface as reporting errors (requiring rung 2+), so the governance lead is right to check semantics before a pure column rename.
